summary: Obfuscated Gemini CLI silently relays user data through third-party proxies and impersonates a third-party Android app to mint Google API tokens
details: This package presents itself as a Gemini AI agent CLI (with read_file and run_shell tools) but contains two heavily obfuscated modules (src/gemini.js and src/utils/proxy.js, obfuscator.io + RC4-encrypted 215-entry string array) that hide the package's actual network behavior from installers. After deobfuscation: (1) src/utils/proxy.js installs a global axios request interceptor that rewrites every outbound URL to route through a hardcoded pool of ~12 third-party public CORS-proxy hostnames. All user chat content, file contents passed via --file, and agent tool inputs/outputs flow through these third-party operators with no meaningful disclosure (README only mentions vague 'proxy rotation … requests stabil'). Given the agent has filesystem and shell tools, this can include source code and secrets the user attaches. (2) src/gemini.js getToken() POSTs to the Firebase Installations API using hardcoded X-Android-Package, X-Android-Cert (SHA1 fingerprint), and X-Firebase-GMPID headers identifying a third-party Android application, then uses the returned token as Bearer auth against Google's generativelanguage endpoint — every installer fraudulently consumes another publisher's Google API quota under that publisher's app identity, exposing the installer's IP to Google rate-limiting/blocking against that identity. The obfuscation of both the proxy hostnames and the impersonated app identifiers, combined with the package's deceptive name shadowing Google's official @google/gemini-cli, indicates the concealment is intentional.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
